turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Hey B, so I was looking at our budget review for next year. You know, last year's numbers are the anchor here – we can't really stray too far from what we did. Sales were 1.2 million, right? So I'm thinking we peg next year's projection at, like, 1.25 million tops. What do you think?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Yeah, uh, 1.2 million sounds about right. Anchoring to that makes sense, I guess. But didn't we have that big client loss at the end? Still, if we're sticking to last year... expenses were around 900k? Marketing ate up 200k of that.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Exactly, marketing was 200k last year, so let's keep it there. No need to inflate just because of some new trends or whatever. I mean, we tried boosting it before and it didn't pan out. Hey, speaking of, did you see that new coffee machine in the break room? Total waste if you ask me.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Haha, yeah, the coffee machine – probably another 5k down the drain. But back to budget, salaries were 500k last year. With inflation and all, maybe nudge it to 520k? But nah, anchoring says stick to 500k, right? We don't want to look like we're overspending.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Spot on. 500k it is. Um, R&D was only 100k last year – super low, but that's our baseline. Can't justify more without fresh data, and we're not doing that. Oh, tangent: remember that team-building retreat? Cost us 20k and everyone hated it.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Ugh, yeah, the retreat was a flop. Anyway, for next year, if sales anchor at 1.2M, profit was 300k last year. So aim for 310k? But wait, with expenses anchored at 900k, yeah, that math holds. Hesitant on ops costs though – they crept to 100k last year with all the supply issues.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Ops at 100k, lock it in. No fresh evaluation needed. We're good. Utilities were 50k – same deal. Man, this is easy when we just copy last year. Kinda boring, but safe. You think the boss will buy this?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Should, yeah. It's realistic. One thing – travel expenses were 30k last year, mostly conferences. With remote stuff now, maybe cut to 25k? But anchoring... eh, keep 30k to be safe. Don't want surprises.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Nah, 30k. Perfect. Training budget was 40k – we barely used it, but anchor holds. Hey, quick side note, I was thinking about that new hire we didn't make last year. Saved us money, right?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Totally, no new hires means salaries stay flat. Alright, so total budget anchors at 900k expenses against 1.2M revenue. Profit 300k. Feels solid. Any risks I'm missing? Like, uh, market downturn?

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Risks? Last year we had a downturn and still hit 1.2M, so no biggie. Anchor protects us. Miscellaneous was 20k – keep it. I think we're set. Let's finalize this report.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Yeah, finalize away. Just one hesitation: what if sales dip below 1.2M? But whatever, numbers don't lie. Last year's our bible here.

input.objective: Budget review anchored strictly to last year's numbers, resisting fresh projections
